data.title: Slower population growth tied to 'historic' migration decline: Census data
data.description:
The U.S. Census Bureau found that a “historic” decline in international migration has contributed greatly to slower population growth in the U.S., according to new data released Tuesday.

Census data shows that population growth increased by 1.8 million, or 0.5 percent, between July 1, 2024, and July 1, 2025. This makes it the slowest population growth since 2021, during the COVID-19 pandemic, when population growth reached 0.2 percent. The overall population reached 341.8 million as of July 1, 2025.

“The slowdown in U.S. population growth is largely due to a historic decline in net international migration, which dropped from 2.7 million to 1.3 million in the period from July 2024 through June 2025,” Christine Hartley, assistant division chief for estimates and projections at the Census Bureau, said in the agency’s report. “With births and deaths remaining relatively stable compared to the prior year, the sharp decline in net international migration is the main reason for the slower growth rate we see today.”

All 50 states and Washington, D.C., saw lower levels of net international migration during the same time period. These levels remained positive across several states, with Florida, Texas, California and New York seeing the highest levels of net international migration.

The Census Bureau projects that net international migration will be at around 321,000 by July 2026 if current trends continue. This would result in another decline of almost 1 million since last July.

The growth caused by net international migration peaked at 2.7 million in 2024. The large drop since then was caused by a decrease in immigration and an increase in people leaving the U.S., or emigration, the Census Bureau stated.

The agency also noted that the data collected “spans two periods of very different immigration policies for the United States,” which the agency said readers should take into “consideration” when looking at the current international migration level.

Net migration per year came out to an average of 2.4 million people between 2021 and 2023, according to The New York Times in December 2024, citing the Congressional Budget Office. The immigration surge under former President Biden became the largest recorded in U.S. history.

President Trump’s return to the White House one month later ushered in a new crackdown on immigration nationwide, with Trump returning to a central issue from his previous administration and campaigns.

Federal immigration enforcement operations, met with pushback from demonstrators in several U.S. cities, have caused increasing tensions with the federal government and local and state leaders across the country. Minnesota is the latest flashpoint on the issue after two U.S. citizens were killed in shootings involving Department of Homeland Security (DHS) officers.

A New York Times/Siena poll released last week, prior to the second fatal shooting, showed that six in 10 Americans disapprove of how Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) is handling its job. This includes 55 percent of registered voters who strongly disapprove and 24 percent who strongly approve.

data.title: Composer Philip Glass cancels Kennedy Center performance
data.description:
Renowned composer Philip Glass has withdrawn his newest symphony from the Kennedy Center, becoming the latest artist step away from the venue in protest of President Trump’s takeover of the performing arts center.

The National Symphony Orchestra was set to premiere Glass’s Symphony No. 15 “Lincoln”, a 40-minute work based on former President Abraham Lincoln’s 1838 Lyceum Address, at the Kennedy Center in June.

However, Glass wrote in a Tuesday statement on Instagram that he decided to withdraw the symphony “after careful consideration,” citing conflicts with the center’s leadership.  

“Symphony No. 15 is a portrait of Abraham Lincoln, and the values of the Kennedy Center today are in direct conflict with the message of the Symphony. Therefore, I feel an obligation to withdraw this Symphony premiere from the Kennedy Center under its current leadership,” Glass wrote.

Glass, who was a Kennedy Center honoree in 2018, is the latest in a growing list of performers who have canceled appearances at the center since Trump took the reins and vowed to fix it early last year and replaced several board members.

Just this month, the center confirmed it agreed to part ways with the Washington National Opera after more than five decades due to a “financially challenging relationship.” Banjo player Béla Fleck and opera singer Renée Fleming also pulled out of upcoming shows.

Ticket sales plummeted in the months that followed, with a Washington Post analysis finding last October that sales had dropped 36 percent compared to the year before, leaving tens of thousands of seats empty at the center’s three main venues.

Dissatisfaction with the direction of the Kennedy Center further grew after the new board, which Trump chairs, voted in November to rename the building the “The Donald J. Trump and the John F. Kennedy Memorial Center for the Performing Arts.”

The center’s interim President Richard Grenell has brushed off the cancellations as “political stunt[s].”

“The artists who are now canceling shows were booked by the previous far left leadership,” he wrote in a December post on the social platform X.

“Their actions prove that the previous team was more concerned about booking far left political activists rather than artists willing to perform for everyone regardless of their political beliefs,” Grenell continued. “Boycotting the Arts to show you support the Arts is a form of derangement syndrome.”

He added, “The arts are for everyone, and the left is mad about it.”

data.title: Megyn Kelly says she doesn't feel sorry for Alex Pretti: 'He was not there to help'
data.description:
Conservative commentator Megyn Kelly on Monday said she doesn’t feel sorry for Alex Pretti, who was fatally shot by a Border Patrol agent in Minnesota on Saturday. 

“I know I’m supposed to feel sorry for Alex Pretty, but I don’t. I don’t. Do you know why I wasn’t shot by Border Patrol this weekend? Because I kept my ass inside and out of their operations.

“It’s very simple,” Kelly said during the Monday episode of “The Megyn Kelly Show.” 

“If I felt strongly enough about something the government was doing, that I would go out and protest, I would do it peacefully on the sidewalk without interfering via a whistle, via shouting, via my body, via any other way,” she added.

Democratic and Republican lawmakers have requested an independent investigation into the incident, the second fatal shooting by a federal officer this month in Minneapolis.

Border Patrol Chief Greg Bovino said Pretti approached officers with a 9 mm handgun, however bystander footage contradicts that claim, showing the 37-year-old ICU nurse holding a cellphone and helping a woman who had been pepper sprayed by a federal officer before being sprayed himself and taken to the ground.

No video disclosed thus far has shown Pretti unholstering his concealed weapon -– which he had a Minnesota permit to carry. Video appears to show one officer taking Pretti’s gun and walking away with it just before shots began.

“If you approach law enforcement with a gun, there is a high likelihood they will be legally justified in shooting you,” U.S. Attorney Bill Essayli wrote in a statement on X.

However, the National Rifle Association (NRA) rejected that argument. 

“This sentiment from the First Assistant U.S. Attorney for the Central District of California is dangerous and wrong,” the NRA wrote in a post addressing Essayli. 

“Responsible public voices should be awaiting a full investigation, not making generalizations and demonizing law-abiding citizens,” the group added.

The Gun Owners of America (GOA) added that the “Second Amendment protects Americans’ right to bear arms while protesting—a right the federal government must not infringe upon.”

Essayli said that GOA mischaracterized his post.

Kelly on Monday urged those protesting the federal immigration crackdown in Minnesota to remain peaceful, adding that “if you do anything, anything that resembles resisting, you’re in serious trouble.”

data.title: Send in Tom Homan! Trump ready to fix Minnesota ICE mess
data.description:
It’s time to get serious. President Trump is deploying border czar Tom Homan to Minnesota, probably to clean up the mess some other members of the administration are making.  

Trump writes on Truth Social: “I am sending Tom Homan to Minnesota tonight. He has not been involved in that area, but knows and likes many of the people there. Tom is tough but fair, and will report directly to me.” 

Trump apparently listened when Brian Kilmeade made this very suggestion on Fox News. And it’s a good one.  

The background to this is that, behind the scenes, there’s tremendous frustration among immigration enforcement officers and other Republican officials with the job that Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem is doing. Under her leadership, immigration enforcement has focused on areas where this is maximum non-compliance from local, Democratic authorities. You can argue these are necessary fights that the administration is picking, but Homan prefers to actually accomplish the job of deporting large numbers of criminal illegal aliens. Maybe it’s time for his approach. 

Noem, on the other hand, keeps making claims she can’t back up, about the motivations of Alex Pretti, who was killed during an altercation with Customs and Border Enforcement. Noem and others are also making the false claim that just carrying a gun is illegal during a protest or a law enforcement encounter, which obviously isn’t true.  

And apparently, Trump is getting fatigued with all this unnecessary controversy. Conservative radio star Eric Erickson writes on X that he had a “Late night phone call with a senior White House official who tells me the President is not happy with how the Department of Homeland Security has handled the response to this incident.” 

I’m glad to hear it, because the fact of the matter is that this incident has not been handled well. As I explained yesterday in my radar, it is shameful that anyone in the administration would consider tossing out the Second Amendment in order to find an excuse not to scrutinize the actions of one specific law enforcement agent. 

Moreover, we need an impartial investigation. We can’t trust what video evidence appears to show us, and apparently we can’t trust what certain top officials are saying. We need to be sober-minded and fair. If there’s a justification for use of force against Pretti, then OK, there’s justification. I can’t tell from the video, and if you think you’re absolutely certain, you’re seeing what you want to see. 

Now the administration has a short window to restore confidence in their operations, which they must do. The American people voted for Trump to carry out deportations of illegal immigrants who are gang members and fraudsters and a net drain on communities. They did not vote for, and will not tolerate, a permanent new police state where American citizens are killed with impunity, and then the administration turns around and lies about the circumstances of those killings.  

We expect, we demand, better from the people running our country. They won’t always get everything right, but we need to have faith that they are trying, and that our liberties — our First and Second Amendment rights — will never be violated. 

If we lose those, then we really don’t have a country anymore. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Ye explains apology for antisemitic remarks: 'Weighing on my spirit'
data.description:
Rapper Kanye West said his apology for previous anti-semetic remarks, published in a full-page ad in The Wall Street Journal on Monday, was motivated by “remorseful feelings” that were “weighing on my spirit,” in an interview published Tuesday in Vanity Fair.

West, who goes by Ye, pushed back on suggestions that the apology was a public relations play as he prepares to release a new album, “Bully,” on Friday, noting that his music has remained popular on Spotify despite the controversies around him.

“This, for me, as evidenced by the letter, isn’t about reviving my commerciality. This is because these remorseful feelings were so heavy on my heart and weighing on my spirit,” he told the outlet. 

“I owe a huge apology once again for everything that I said that hurt the Jewish and Black communities in particular. All of it went too far,” he added.

“As a public figure, so many people follow and listen to my every word. It’s important that they realize and understand what side of history that I want to stand on. And that is one of love and positivity.”

In his apology published in the Wall Street Journal, Ye said that a car accident that broke his jaw 25 years ago also caused brain damage, which went undiagnosed until 2023. Ye was first diagnosed with bipolar disorder in 2016.

Bipolar disorder, Ye said, makes “you feel like you’re seeing the world more clearly than ever, when in reality you’re losing your grip entirely.”

In recent years, Ye has made frequent highly inflammatory antisemitic remarks.

In a 2022 interview, he praised Adolf Hitler, saying he “liked” the Nazi leader. That same year, his account on the social platform X was temporarily suspended after he posted that he was “going death con 3 on JEWISH PEOPLE.” Last year, Shopify shuttered Ye’s online store that was linked to his website, which was selling $20 T-shirts bearing large swastika symbols. 

Ye blamed his behavior on a “manic episode” resulting from his disorder in the advertisement and interview with Vanity Fair.

“When you’re manic, you really don’t think that you’re sick. You think that everyone else is deeply overreacting. You feel like you’re seeing the world so much more clearly on things, when in reality you’re losing your grip entirely. That’s what it felt like at that time,” he told Vanity Fair.

Ye said he’s now on antipsychotic medications, which initially sent him into a deep depression.

“Finding the right dosage is difficult, but it’s important and critical to finding the right balance with the illness. Zombifying otherwise becomes a side effect of a high dosage. The side effects as such have been a reality for me at times,” he said.

“I’m just trying to find what works for me so that I can continue down this positive course,” he added.

data.title: TikTok settles social media addiction lawsuit ahead of trial
data.description:
TikTok has settled a lawsuit accusing it and several other social media companies of designing addictive platforms shortly before the landmark case headed to trial Tuesday, according to an attorney for the plaintiffs.

It is the latest company to settle the case and avoid trial, after Snapchat’s parent company similarly reached a settlement last week. The trial will now go forward with the two remaining companies, Meta and YouTube.

Thousands of individuals, school districts and states have filed lawsuits against the four social media companies, which have since been consolidated into two sprawling cases. The case in Los Angeles Superior Court is the first to head to trial on a series of “bellwether” or test cases.

This story will be updated.

data.title: A Trump-appointed judge taught Lindsey Halligan a lesson she won't forget
data.description:
What happens when a Trump-appointed U.S. Attorney addresses a federal judge in the same contemptuous manner that Trump uses with anyone he doesn’t like? 

She gets her head handed to her.

 Last year President Trump appointed Lindsey Halligan — then a senior White House aide with no prosecutorial experience — as interim U.S. Attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia. Her chief qualification seemed to be enthusiasm for Trump’s vengeance agenda.

She promptly indicted former FBI Director James Comey and New York’s attorney general Letitia James, even though career prosecutors had warned her that the evidence in their cases — that Comey had lied to Congress and that James had committed mortgage fraud — was insufficient for a successful prosecution. A federal judge then ruled that Trump had appointed Halligan illegally and dismissed the indictments.

Despite the ruling that she was serving unlawfully, Halligan remained in office for two more months, continuing to sign filings as the U.S. Attorney and draw a federal salary.

U.S. District Judge David Novak, a Trump appointee, who presided over an indictment with Halligan’s name in the signature block, ordered her to explain why she was still on the premises of the U.S. Attorney’s office. In response to that straightforward question, Halligan called Judge Novak’s order “an inquisition,” an “insult,” a “gross abuse of power” and a “rudimentary” legal error.

Federal judges demand that the attorneys who appear before them respect the courts and their orders. Anything less shows contempt for the law, which they have the authority to severely punish, including by imprisonment.

After reading her statement, Judge Novak gave a brutal master class to Halligan on how federal prosecutors should behave.

Her response, he wrote, “contains a level of vitriol more appropriate for a cable news talk show and fell below the level expected from litigants, particularly the Department of Justice.”  She had shown intolerable “obstinance” by ignoring a district court order. “If the court were to allow Ms. Halligan and the Department of Justice to pick and choose which orders that they will follow, the same would have to be true for other litigants and our system of justice would crumble.“ 

Novak was just warming up. He observed that he had once sent a defense attorney to prison for six months for defying court orders, and he seemed ready to start disciplinary proceedings against Halligan. “This charade of Ms. Halligan masquerading as the United States Attorney for this District must come to an end.” In the end, although Novak granted Halligan the “benefit of the doubt” and refrained from disciplinary action “at this time,” his justification for leniency was itself a damning rebuke.

“Ms. Halligan lacks the prosecutorial experience,” Judge Novak wrote, “that has long been the norm for those nominated to the position of United States Attorney in this District.”  In other words, her excuse is that she had no idea what she was doing.

Within hours, Halligan resigned. She got what she deserved — a stunningly bad public judicial fitness report that will haunt her career. But it hardly slows the downward spiral of the once-admired Department of Justice into corruption and incompetence, where political hacks like Halligan who have no respect for the law replace the experienced, able prosecutors who do.

In his opinion, Novak quoted another federal judge frustrated with the Trump administration’s contempt for the law: “We yet cling to the hope that it is not naive to believe our good Brethren in the executive branch perceive the rule of law as vital to the American ethos.”   

Until that actually happens, Justice Department prosecutors like Lindsey Halligan should be warned: The federal judges are angry. 

Gregory J. Wallance was a federal prosecutor in the Carter and Reagan administrations and a member of the ABSCAM prosecution team, which convicted a U.S. senator and six representatives of bribery. He is the author of “Into Siberia: George Kennan’s Epic Journey Through the Brutal, Frozen Heart of Russia.”

data.title: 6K+ people killed in Iran crackdown, activists say
data.description:
Over 6,000 people have been killed amid the Iranian government’s crackdown on recent protests in the country, an activist group said on Tuesday. 

The protests began on Dec. 28 in response to the plummeting value of the rial, Iranian currency. The government responded to the protests with the bloodiest crackdown in several decades in the country and a two-week internet blackout. 

The Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA), an independent U.S.-based advocacy organization for human rights in Iran, said that 6,159 people are reported dead. 

The group said that 5,804 demonstrators, 214 government-affiliated forces, 92 children and 49 civilians who were not participating in the protests are included in this total.

The agency sources its information from a network of activists in the country, and The Associated Press noted that HRANA’s death counts in previous Iranian conflicts have been accurate. 

HRANA’s report is double the number provided by the Iranian government, which said last week that 3,117 people had been killed during protests in the country. Iran has a history of undercounting death tolls. 

In an op-ed published by The Wall Street Journal last week, Iran Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi pushed back on what he called “lies being peddled abroad” about the government’s crackdown on protests, and he sharply criticized the Trump administration’s threats to intervene if the government killed protesters.

President Trump said last Thursday that the U.S. has a “massive fleet” heading to the Middle East to prepare for a potential intervention. The USS Abraham Lincoln aircraft carrier and American warships arrived in the region on Monday.

“We have a big force going toward Iran,” Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One last Thursday. “I’d rather not see anything happen, but we’re watching them very closely.”

On Saturday, Iranian Revolutionary Guard Commander Mohammad Pakpour said the country’s military has its “finger on the trigger” if the U.S. decides to respond.

data.title: Paul calls for agents involved in Minneapolis shooting to be put on leave
data.description:
Senate Homeland Security Committee Chair Rand Paul (R-Ky.) says the Border Patrol agents involved in the fatal shooting of 37-year-old nurse Alex Pretti in Minneapolis should be put on administrative leave immediately.

“Local police routinely put officers involved in deadly shootings on administrative leave until an independent investigation is concluded. That should happen immediately,” Paul posted on X, the social media site.

“I can’t recall ever hearing a police chief immediately describing the victim as a ‘domestic terrorist’ or a ‘would-be assassin.’ For calm to be restored, an independent investigation is the least that should be done,” Paul wrote, making a pointed reference to Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and senior White House adviser Stephen Miller, who described Pretti in those terms immediately after the shooting.

The Homeland Security Investigations office is leading the review of the shooting but some prominent commentators such as Bill O’Reilly, who spoke on Monday to NewsNation’s Leland Vittert, are calling for the FBI to take the lead.

Paul is the latest of many Republican senators who have called for an independent investigation of the shooting after federal officials barred investigators from the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension from reviewing the scene of the shooting.

A federal district judge in Minnesota on Saturday issued an order directing the Department of Homeland Security to preserve evidence linked to Pretti’s shooting.

Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and state Attorney General Keith Ellison say that state investigators were denied access to the scene even after obtaining a judicial warrant.

Noem said earlier this month that state investigators didn’t have the jurisdiction to investigate the scene of the fatal shooting of another protester, Renee Good, also 37, who was shot in her car by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer.

Paul sent letters Monday to the heads of ICE, Customs and Border Protection and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services calling on them to testify before his committee at an open hearing scheduled for Feb. 12.

Other Republicans have called for a thorough and independent investigation of Saturday’s shooting.

“Any loss of life under politically charged circumstances is a tragedy. I support a full and impartial investigation into the events leading up to the death of Alex Pretti,” said Senate Finance Committee Chairman Mike Crapo (R-Idaho), who doesn’t often weigh in on explosive news-of-the-day controversies not related to his panel’s jurisdiction over taxes and entitlement programs.  

Other Republicans who have called for a thorough and impartial investigation include Sens. Dave McCormick (Pa.), Jon Husted (Ohio), Thom Tillis (N.C.) and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska).

data.title: Homeland Security Democrats: Noem will be impeached if she is not fired, resigns
data.description:
House Homeland Security Committee Democrats said Tuesday that Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem is going to be impeached if she is not fired or steps out of her role.

“Kristi Noem has destroyed public confidence in her ability to lead [the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).] She lied about Alex Pretti,” the panel’s Democrats posted Tuesday on the social platform X.

“She has been rebuked by Republicans in Congress, by her own senior staffers, and even the President. She needs to be fired, resign, or she will be impeached,” they added.

The post comes after intense backlash toward the Trump administration over the fatal shootings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good in Minneapolis. Both were shot and killed by federal immigration officers.

Noem had said that Pretti “attacked” federal law enforcement and was “brandishing” a firearm, calling the incident an example of “domestic terrorism.” She made similar comments about Good earlier this month.

The House Democrats join Senate voices in calling for action on Noem.

Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) said she committed an “impeachable” offense by calling Pretti a “domestic terrorist.”

And centrist Democratic Sen. John Fetterman (Pa.) called on Trump her. “President Trump: I make a direct appeal to immediately fire Secretary Noem. Americans have died. She is betraying DHS’s core mission and trashing your border security legacy,” Fetterman said in a statement.

But President Trump on Tuesday, when asked if Noem will step down, responded, “No.”

Noem has agreed to testify in front of the Senate Judiciary Committee in a little over a month, testimony that was requested before the incidents in Minneapolis. 

Senate Homeland Security Committee Chair Rand Paul (R-Ky.) has also sent letters to the leaders of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) to testify in front of the upper chamber’s Homeland Security Committee.

The Kentucky Republican told the leaders of President Trump’s immigration agencies that “Congress has an obligation to conduct oversight” of what he referred to as the “exceptional amount of funding” the Republican Congress has set aside to secure borders and enforce immigration law.

The Hill has reached out to DHS for comment.
